is a monument on the [Missouri River](/wiki/Missouri_River "Missouri River") at [Floyd's Bluff](/wiki/Floyd%27s_Bluff "Floyd's Bluff") in [Sioux City, Iowa](/wiki/Sioux_City,_Iowa "Sioux City, Iowa"), US. From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Monument in Iowa, US, to honor Charles Floyd of the Lewis and Clark Expedition

United States historic place

The **Sergeant Floyd Monument** is a monument on the [Missouri River](/wiki/Missouri_River "Missouri River") at [Floyd's Bluff](/wiki/Floyd%27s_Bluff "Floyd's Bluff") in [Sioux City, Iowa](/wiki/Sioux_City,_Iowa "Sioux City, Iowa"), US. The monument honors [Charles Floyd](/wiki/Charles_Floyd_(explorer) "Charles Floyd (explorer)"), a member of the [Lewis and Clark Expedition](/wiki/Lewis_and_Clark_Expedition "Lewis and Clark Expedition"), who died on the upstream voyage in 1804 and was buried here.

The monument is the first designated [National Historic Landmark](/wiki/National_Historic_Landmark "National Historic Landmark") of the United States.

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Sergeant_Floyd_Monument&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

Charles Floyd (1782–1804) was a United States [explorer](/wiki/Explorer "Explorer"), a non-commissioned officer and [quartermaster](/wiki/Quartermaster "Quartermaster") in the [Lewis and Clark Expedition](/wiki/Lewis_and_Clark_Expedition "Lewis and Clark Expedition"). A native of [Kentucky](/wiki/Kentucky "Kentucky"), he was a relative of [William Clark](/wiki/William_Clark_(explorer) "William Clark (explorer)"). He was one of the first men to join the expedition.

While exploring the [Louisiana Purchase](/wiki/Louisiana_Purchase "Louisiana Purchase") with Lewis and Clark, Floyd took ill at the end of July 1804. On July 31, Floyd wrote in his diary, "I am very sick and has been for sometime but have recovered my health again." However, this apparent recovery was soon followed by a severe turn for the worse. William Clark described his colleague's death as one "with a great deal of composure", and said that before Floyd died, he told Clark: "I am going away. I want you to write me a letter." The sergeant died on August 20. The expedition held a funeral and buried Floyd on a bluff overlooking the [Missouri River](/wiki/Missouri_River "Missouri River"); they named it Floyd's Bluff in his honor.

Clark diagnosed Floyd's illness as [bilious colic](/wiki/Abdominal_pain "Abdominal pain"), though modern doctors and historians agree Floyd's death was more likely to have been caused by a ruptured [appendix](/wiki/Vermiform_appendix "Vermiform appendix"). The brief "recovery" Floyd described may have represented the temporary relief afforded by the bursting of the organ, which would have been followed by a fatal [peritonitis](/wiki/Peritonitis "Peritonitis"). In those days, appendicitis was almost invariably fatal; while appendectomies had been performed as early as 1735, they were extremely hazardous and agonizing, and unfamiliar to most doctors of the era.

By 1857, [erosion](/wiki/Erosion "Erosion") had caused much of Floyd's grave—even the original [cedar post marker](/wiki/Gravestone "Gravestone") left by the crew of the expedition—to slide into the river and wash away. Concerned citizens rescued most of his skeleton, including his skull, and re-buried it 200 meters east of the original burial site. A forensic reconstruction of Sgt. Floyd's probable facial appearance based on a plaster cast of his skull is on display at the [Sergeant Floyd Riverboat Museum](/wiki/Sergeant_Floyd_(towboat) "Sergeant Floyd (towboat)") in Sioux City.

After Floyd's expedition journal was published in 1894, new interest was taken in him and his remains were buried again on August 20, 1895, the anniversary of his death. Sioux City residents Thomas J. Stone, John H. Charles, George D. Perkins, C. R. Marks, and G. W. Wakefield established the Floyd Association to erect a monument.[[3]](#cite_note-3)[[4]](#cite_note-:0-4) It took five years to raise $20,000 and development began in May 1900 with the pouring of a concrete base.[[4]](#cite_note-:0-4) Floyd's remains were reinterred for the final time on August 20, 1900, the anniversary of his death. The obelisk of white sandstone standing 100 feet (30 m) high was completed on May 30, 1901. In 1960, the monument was recognized by the [U.S. Department of Interior](/wiki/U.S._Department_of_Interior "U.S. Department of Interior") as the first [National Historic Landmark](/wiki/National_Historic_Landmark "National Historic Landmark"). It was designated a National Historic Landmark on June 30, 1960.[[2]](#cite_note-nhlsum-2)[[5]](#cite_note-nrhpinv2-5)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/bb/Lewis_clark_Boyer_soldier_littlesioux_floyd.jpg/250px-Lewis_clark_Boyer_soldier_littlesioux_floyd.jpg)](/wiki/File:Lewis_clark_Boyer_soldier_littlesioux_floyd.jpg)

This excerpt from the Lewis and Clark map of 1814 shows the rivers of western Iowa. Floyd's Grave is noted at the left of the map.

The Floyd Monument is now within a 23-acre (93,000 m2) park that overlooks the Missouri River valley.[[6]](#cite_note-6) Floyd's final resting place is located on old [U.S. Highway 75](/wiki/U.S._Route_75 "U.S. Route 75"), in the southern part of Sioux City.
